Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Anthropic Claude agent framework "harness" and related npm packages:

## Claude Agent SDK (Agent Harness)

The agent harness that powers Claude Code (the Claude Code SDK) can power many other types of agents, too. To reflect this broader vision, Anthropic renamed the Claude Code SDK to the **Claude Agent SDK**.

### NPM Installation

The package can be installed via npm:
```bash
npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk
```

The Claude Agent SDK is available in multiple forms to suit different use cases: TypeScript SDK for Node.js and web applications, Python SDK for Python applications and data science, and supports both streaming and single mode.

### What is the "Harness"?

An **Agent Harness** is defined as an external set of functionality to enhance a model's runtime execution. Examples include: (1) conversation & context management, (2) a tool invocation layer (MCP/SDK tools), (3) permissions, (4) session & file-system state, (5) loop control & error handling, (6) basic observability/telemetry.(no content)Built on top of the agent harness that powers Claude Code, the Claude Agent SDK provides all the building blocks you need to build production-ready agents, including: Context Management (automatic compaction), Rich tool ecosystem (file operations, code execution, web search, MCP extensibility), Advanced permissions, and Production essentials like built-in error handling, session management, and monitoring.

### Key Features

Claude Agent SDK supports subagents by default. Subagents are useful for parallelization (spinning up multiple subagents to work on different tasks simultaneously) and managing context (subagents use their own isolated context windows and only send relevant information back to the orchestrator).

### Authentication

For basic authentication, retrieve a Claude API key from the Claude Console and set the `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` environment variable. The SDK also supports authentication via Amazon Bedrock and Google Vertex AI.

content.content:
# Claude Agent SDK: Installation and Usage Guide

Based on the article, here's what's available about the Claude Agent SDK:

## Getting Started

The article provides limited installation details. It states you can **"get started with the Claude Agent SDK today"** by visiting the documentation, with a migration guide available for existing developers.

## Key Capabilities Rather Than Code Examples

The article focuses on architectural patterns rather than code snippets. It emphasizes:

- **Agent Loop Pattern**: "gather context -> take action -> verify work -> repeat"
- **Computer Access**: Agents receive "tools to run bash commands, edit files, create files and search files"

## Primary Building Blocks

The SDK provides these fundamental components:

1. **Tools** - Custom actions agents prioritize in their context window
2. **Bash & Scripts** - General-purpose command execution
3. **Code Generation** - Python script creation for complex operations
4. **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** - Pre-built integrations (Slack, GitHub, Asana, etc.)

## Documentation References

The article directs readers to:
- Official documentation at `docs.claude.com/en/api/agent-sdk/overview`
- Migration guide for existing SDK users
- Blog post on "Writing effective tools for agents"

**Note**: This article is architectural guidance rather than a technical installation manual. Actual code examples and detailed setup instructions are in the linked documentation.

content.thinking: Projeto criado com sucesso. Vou mostrar um resumo ao usuário.

content.text:
Projeto **MD (macOS DevTools)** criado com sucesso.

## Estrutura do Projeto

```
md/
├── src/
│   ├── agent/
│   │   └── index.ts      # MDAgent - agente principal com Claude SDK
│   ├── tools/
│   │   ├── bash.ts       # Tool para executar comandos bash
│   │   └── finder.ts     # Tool para controlar o Finder via AppleScript
│   └── index.ts          # Entry point e CLI
├── package.json
├── tsconfig.json
├── bun.lock
└── .gitignore
```

## Funcionalidades Implementadas

1. **Agente Claude** - Usa o SDK oficial da Anthropic com padrão de tool use
2. **Bash Tool** - Executa comandos no terminal, rastreia `cd` para manter o diretório atual
3. **Finder Tool** - Sincroniza o Finder com o diretório via AppleScript
   - Quando você executa `cd`, a janela do Finder muda automaticamente

## Como Usar

```bash
# Configurar API key
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sua-chave"

# Executar
bun run start
```

## Próximos Passos

Para transformar isso em uma barra lateral no Finder, existem algumas opções:

1. **Finder Sync Extension** (nativo) - Requer desenvolvimento em Swift
2. **App Auxiliar** - Um app que fica como sidebar flutuante
3. **Menu Bar App** - App na barra de menu com popover

Qual abordagem você prefere seguir para a interface visual?

Here are the search results for macOS window decoration, custom toolbar, SIMBL, and mach_inject plugins:

## SIMBL Overview

SIMBL (a.k.a. Simple Bundle Loader and Smart Input Manager Bundle Loader) allows developers to make modifications to programs written in Cocoa without access to the source code.(no content)Originally, SIMBL was used to modify Apple's Safari browser (once known as PithHelmet), which didn't have an extension infrastructure until 2010.(no content)Today, SIMBL is a platform for modifying how programs run on your Mac. It can inject changes into other applications, allowing you to change how applications look and run. Fundamentally, you can imagine SIMBL as a package manager for application plug-ins.

## Plugin Managers

- **mySIMBL** - A plugin manager for macOS.(no content)By itself, mySIMBL is only a framework for managing plugins. It doesn't change any functionality, but it provides the tool that allows you to install plugins.

- **MacForge** - A platform with plugins for "dynamic introspection, manipulation and scripting of Cocoa objects on macOS 10.10+" using technologies including mach-inject.

## Window Decoration Plugins

- **Black Mac OS X** - A free, open-source SIMBL plugin created by Alex Zielenski. In a finished state it will provide a complete Mac OS X Snow Leopard theme and hopefully 10.7 Lion.

- **SIMBLPlugins Collection** - GlobalNoWindowIcon hides the application icon in the title bar.(no content)Menufela will automatically hide and show the dock and menubar per app in cocoa based applications. Caffo's version of Menufela additionally adds a border around windows and hides the titlebar.

- **MenuBarHider** - This plugin will do nothing to Carbon-based applications, since SIMBL will only inject code into Cocoa applications.(no content)To install this plugin, either clone the git repository and use XCode to build the bundle, or get a precompiled bundle from the downloads section on GitHub. Then put the bundle into "~/Library/Application Support/SIMBL/Plugins".

## Installation

Grab a copy of Easy SIMBL and import a plugin.(no content)If you had installed EasySIMBL before, just restart your applications. If you don't have EasySIMBL on your machine yet, get it from https://github.com/norio-nomura/EasySIMBL and follow the instructions there to install.

Optimized for M1/M2 Macs and macOS Ventura/Sonoma.","url":"https://github.com/TotalFinder-macOS/totalfinder-osx"},{"title":"XtraFinder - TotalFinder Alternative - Best Mac Tips","url":"https://best-mac-tips.com/2014/02/07/xtrafinder-totalfinder-alternative/"}]

Based on the search results, here's how TotalFinder and XtraFinder work by injecting into macOS Finder:

## How TotalFinder/XtraFinder Injection Works

### TotalFinder's Injection Mechanism

TotalFinder is installed as a standalone application. But when launched it changes the current instance of Finder running in memory. This process is called "TotalFinder injection into Finder."(no content)When launched, it changes the current instance of Finder running in memory. In other words, TotalFinder performs code injection into Finder.(no content)TotalFinder needs to send Apple Events to inject its own code into running Finder.app and then successfully take control over it.

### Security Requirements

In order to inject the XtraFinder features into Finder, you need to disable System Integrity Protection (SIP) on your Mac. How much of a risk this is will depend on how you use your Mac and the kinds of apps you install on it.(no content)SIP must remain weakened while using TotalFinder, and Apple Silicon compatibility must be manually enabled.

### Historical Context

In the old days when TotalFinder was first written macOS was pretty unrestricted. Apps could talk to each other freely. It was flexible but potentially unsecure. Over time Apple devs started adding various ways to harden the system. In recent macOS versions user has to grant permissions to TotalFinder to be able to talk to Finder using Apple Events.

### Risks and Considerations

Due to the nature of how TotalFinder works, there is always a minor risk that TotalFinder might break with future Finder updates.(no content)XtraFinder isn't a Finder replacement, but rather, it modifies Finder itself to add new features and capabilities that make it far better for managing files. It can be a more elegant solution than installing a separate app, though there is some risk involved in using it.

### Features Added via Injection

Both tools add similar features through this injection method:
- Tabs, Dual Panel, and numerous features for macOS's native Finder with support for macOS Ventura, Intel and Apple silicon.
- Tabbed browsing allows multiple folders to be opened in a single window for quick switching. The dual-pane mode enables side-by-side folder comparisons, drag-and-drop file transfers, and multi-folder operations.

Based on my search, here's what I found regarding injecting custom UI into the macOS Finder window sidebar:

## Official Apple Approach: Finder Sync Extensions

With a Finder Sync extension you register one or more folders for the system to monitor. Your Finder Sync extension then sets badges, labels, and contextual menus for any items in the monitored folders. You can also use the extension point's API to add a toolbar button to the Finder window or a sidebar icon for the monitored folder.(no content)The FIFinderSync protocol declares a number of methods that you can implement to monitor and control the Finder. These methods let you receive notifications when the user observes monitored items, add contextual menus to monitored items, and add custom toolbar and sidebar icons.

### Custom Sidebar Icons
You can provide a custom sidebar icon for any of the root folders your extension is monitoring. If the user drags one of these root folders into the Finder's sidebar, your icon will be displayed instead of the default folder icon. To provide a custom sidebar icon, add the icons to your containing app. For this to work, both your app's icons and the sidebar icons must be included in an iconset. If you are using an asset catalog to manage your app's icons, you will need to switch to an iconset.

## Third-Party Resources

- There's a GitHub project called "custom-finder-sidebar-icons" - an Xcode project to add custom sidebar icons to folders on macOS.

- There's also a blog post about setting custom sidebar icons for folders in macOS, where the author "managed to get this working with a lot of trial and error."

The search results didn't surface any specific "hacks" for deeply injecting custom UI elements into Finder beyond what the official Finder Sync extension API provides. The official API is fairly limited—it allows badges, contextual menus, toolbar buttons, and custom sidebar icons for monitored folders, but doesn't provide full access to inject arbitrary UI into Finder windows.

MacForge is an open-source plugin manager for macOS. It lets you discover, install and manage plugins to improve the user experience of macOS without the need for manually cloning/building or copying files.

### Key Features

MacForge lets you discover and manage awesome plugins to enhance your macOS experience. There are dozens of plugins to discover and you can even create your own. The selection of plugins offered through MacForge is always expanding.(no content)You can use the tool to install, delete, enable and disable plugins. Additionally, you can prevent some applications from loading plugins by creating a sort of blacklist.

### Plugin Injection

The SIMBLAgent (injector) is now the MacForgeHelper, which is in charge of injection. The helper automatically tries to load new bundles when the main app is closed and checks for bundle updates in background.

### Example Use Cases

Users use it to allow colored icons in the Finder sidebar and number badges in trash.

### Installation

MacForge is available via Homebrew as a cask from the official site at macenhance.com/macforge.

### Current Status

The latest version of MacForge is 1.1.(no content)The product is open-source and available at no cost, with a community of developers supporting MacForge.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Finder Sync Extensions and custom sidebar functionality:

## Finder Sync Extension Capabilities

Finder Sync Extensions let you receive notifications when the user observes monitored items, add contextual menus to monitored items, and add custom toolbar and sidebar icons.

### Core Features
With a Finder Sync extension you register one or more folders for the system to monitor. Your Finder Sync extension then sets badges, labels, and contextual menus for any items in the monitored folders. You can also use the extension point's API to add a toolbar button to the Finder window or a sidebar icon for the monitored folder.

### Custom Sidebar Icons
You can provide a custom sidebar icon for any of the root folders your extension is monitoring. If the user drags one of these root folders into the Finder's sidebar, your icon will be displayed instead of the default folder icon. To provide a custom sidebar icon, add the icons to your containing app.(no content)For this to work, both your app's icons and the sidebar icons must be included in an iconset. If you are using an asset catalog to manage your app's icons, you will need to switch to an iconset.

### Limitations
**Important:** The Finder Sync API does **not** support adding a custom view panel or inspector sidebar to Finder. The API is limited to:
- Badges for sync state
- Contextual menus
- Toolbar buttons
- Custom sidebar icons (for folder shortcuts only)

The Finder Sync extension point lets you modify an item's appearance in the Finder. It does not provide support for syncing the files.

## Example Projects

Several open-source Swift projects demonstrate Finder Sync implementations:

1. **FinderEx** (GitHub: yantoz/FinderEx) - Serves as a playground to learn Swift, Cocoa, Interface Builder, Delegations, Controllers, FinderSync, XPC and all other Mac specific things.

2. **FiScript** - Execute custom scripts from the MacOS context menu (CTRL+click) in Finder.

3. **custom-finder-sidebar-icons** - An Xcode project to set custom icons for folders in the Finder sidebar, based on documentation about CFBundleSymbolName.

If you need a true custom view panel in Finder, you would likely need to explore private APIs (not recommended for App Store apps) or create a separate companion window that syncs with Finder selection.

Agora tenho bastante informação. Deixa eu compilar um plano claro para o usuário.

Resumo das opções:

1. **Finder Sync Extension (oficial Apple)** - Limitado a badges, menus contextuais, toolbar buttons e ícones sidebar. NÃO permite adicionar UI customizada.

2. **Code Injection (TotalFinder/XtraFinder style)** - Usa técnicas como SIMBL/MacForge/mach_inject para injetar código no Finder. Requer desabilitar SIP (System Integrity Protection). Não funciona em Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3).

3. **MacForge plugins** - Similar ao anterior, requer SIP desabilitado e não suporta Apple Silicon.

4. **App nativo separado** - Criar um app Swift que replica funcionalidades do Finder com terminal integrado. Mais seguro e compatível.

5. **Janela flutuante "ancorada"** - Uma janela transparente/borderless que fica posicionada ao lado do Finder e sincroniza via polling.

Vou apresentar isso ao usuário de forma clara.

Here are the search results for creating floating windows in macOS with NSWindow and Swift:

## Key Findings

### Basic Floating Window Setup

To keep an NSWindow always on top, you can override `windowDidLoad()` and set `window?.level = .floating`(no content)Before launching the window, just use the appropriate window level. For example, `window.level = NSWindow.Level.popUpMenu` or for a higher level: `window.level = NSWindow.Level.screenSaver`

### Window Level Options

Common window levels include:
- `NSWindow.Level.normal` - Regular window
- `NSWindow.Level.floating` - Floating window
- `NSWindow.Level.popUpMenu` - Menu level
- `NSWindow.Level.modalPanel` - Modal dialog level
- `NSWindow.Level.screenSaver` - Screen saver level

### SwiftUI Approach (macOS 15+)

When developing macOS applications with SwiftUI, you might need to create floating windows that stay on top of other windows. While modern macOS versions (15+) make this straightforward with the `.windowLevel(.floating)` modifier, supporting older versions requires a different approach.

### Supporting Older macOS Versions

To support older macOS versions, we need to access the underlying NSWindow and set its level manually using a `WindowAccessor: NSViewRepresentable` that provides a callback to access the window.

### Fullscreen & Collection Behavior

For windows that need to appear on all spaces, you can use `window.collectionBehavior = [.canJoinAllSpaces]`

### Window Positioning

You can make an NSViewRepresentable in the background of the SwiftUI view and access the window property, then put this inside a ViewModifier to control window positioning.

### Note on Anchoring to Another Window

The search results don't show a direct API for "anchoring" one window to another. However, you would typically need to:
1. Observe the parent window's frame changes using `NSWindow.didMoveNotification` or `NSWindow.didResizeNotification`
2. Update the child window's position accordingly using `window.setFrameOrigin()` or similar methods

content.text:
## Plano: MD (macOS DevTools)

### Resumo da Pesquisa

Sem hacks do SO, as opções oficiais são:

| Abordagem | Viabilidade | Limitações |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Finder Sync Extension** | ❌ Inviável | Só permite badges, menus contextuais e ícones. Não permite adicionar UI/views customizadas |
| **Janela flutuante ancorada** | ⚠️ Parcial | Não existe API para "grudar" janelas. Seria posicionamento manual via polling |
| **App nativo Swift** | ✅ Viável | Não é o Finder real, mas pode ter a mesma UI + terminal integrado |

---

### Plano Proposto: App Nativo Swift

Criar um **file browser nativo** em Swift/SwiftUI que:

1. **Replica a UI do Finder** (sidebar, lista de arquivos, toolbar)
2. **Adiciona painel de terminal** no lado direito (como na sua imagem)
3. **Sincroniza navegação** - `cd` no terminal muda a pasta, clicar em pasta muda o `pwd`
4. **Usa APIs nativas** do macOS (NSFileManager, FSEvents para watch)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ← →  │ ~/Projects/md                      │ ≡  ⊞     │
├───────┼─────────────────────────────┬───────────────────┤
│ Favoritos │                         │ ~/Projects/md     │
│  📁 Desktop│  Name          Size    │ $ ls              │
│  📁 Docs   │  📁 src        --      │ src/ package.json │
│  📁 Down.. │  📄 package    2KB     │ $ cd src          │
│            │  📄 tsconfig   1KB     │ $ _               │
│ Locations  │                        │                   │
│  💻 Mac    │                        │                   │
│  ☁️ iCloud │                        │                   │
└───────┴─────────────────────────────┴───────────────────┘
```

---

### Stack Tecnológico

- **SwiftUI** - UI declarativa
- **AppKit** (NSWorkspace, NSFileManager) - Operações de arquivo
- **FSEvents** - Watch de mudanças no filesystem
- **Pseudoterminal (PTY)** - Terminal real integrado (não emulador fake)

---

### Fases de Desenvolvimento

**Fase 1: MVP**
- File browser básico com navegação
- Terminal integrado com PTY
- Sincronização bidirecional

**Fase 2: Paridade com Finder**
- Drag & drop
- Quick Look (preview)
- Ícones e thumbnails
- Atalhos de teclado

**Fase 3: Extras**
- Abas/tabs
- Split view
- Integração Git (status dos arquivos)

---

Confirma essa abordagem para eu iniciar?

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - PabloLION/xterm-react: React component for xterm.js terminal integration","url":"https://github.com/PabloLION/xterm-react/"},{"title":"react-xtermjs - a React Library to Build Terminals","url":"https://www.qovery.com/blog/react-xtermjs-a-react-library-to-build-terminals"},{"title":"GitHub - Qovery/react-xtermjs: Xterm.js for React","url":"https://github.com/Qovery/react-xtermjs"},{"title":"Xterm.js","url":"https://xtermjs.org/"},{"title":"GitHub - robert-harbison/xterm-for-react: A React wrapper made for XTerm.js","url":"https://github.com/robert-harbison/xterm-for-react"},{"title":"Easy Web Terminal Magic: Integrating xterm.js with React","url":"https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/easy-web-terminal-magic-integrating-xtermjs-react-john-kagunda-545gf"},{"title":"@pablo-lion/xterm-react - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/@pablo-lion/xterm-react"},{"title":"xterm-for-react - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/xterm-for-react"},{"title":"GitHub - xtermjs/xterm.js: A terminal for the web","url":"https://github.com/xtermjs/xterm.js"},{"title":"xterm - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/xterm"}]

Here are the search results for xterm.js terminal emulator TypeScript React integration:

## Overview

Xterm.js is a front-end component written in TypeScript that lets applications bring fully-featured terminals to their users in the browser. It's used by popular projects such as VS Code, Hyper and Theia.

## React Integration Libraries

### 1. **react-xtermjs** (by Qovery)
Qovery built react-xtermjs because they "didn't find a React library that fit [their] needs" when adding a terminal to their UI Console. It's based on the popular XTerm.js library.(no content)Existing libraries like xterm-for-react and react-xterm "have not been updated for many years and do not support hooks and components, limiting their flexibility and usability."(no content)The useXTerm hook allows more flexibility with the underlying XTerm.js instance.

### 2. **@pablo-lion/xterm-react**
This project provides a React component that integrates the xterm.js terminal emulator library, aiming to offer a more up-to-date and flexible alternative to existing solutions.

Key features:
- Utilizes the latest @xterm/xterm version to address issues with deprecated add-ons, such as the non-functional xterm-addon-fit in resizing terminals.
- Newer React and TypeScript for peer dependencies.
- Exposes terminal methods like .write(), .blur(), etc., directly from the XTerm class, enhancing developer experience and code readability.

### 3. **xterm-for-react**
XTerm For React is a React wrapper made for XTerm.js to allow you to easily integrate XTerm into any React project.(no content)If there's no wrapper for a specific method in XTerm.js, you can call any function in XTerm.js using a ref.

## Key Features of xterm.js

- Terminal apps just work: Xterm.js works with most terminal apps such as bash, vim, and tmux, including support for curses-based apps and mouse events.
- Performant: Xterm.js is really fast, it even includes a GPU-accelerated renderer.
- Rich Unicode support including CJK, emojis, and IMEs. It's self-contained with zero dependencies, and has accessibility features like screen reader support.

## API & TypeScript Support

The full API for xterm.js is contained within the TypeScript declaration file.(no content)Xterm.js follows a monthly release cycle roughly.

Here are the search results for Tauri file browser with macOS native look using React components:

## File Browser Tutorial
There's a tutorial on how to build a basic File Browser using Tauri, React, and Typescript. This guide covers setting up the project, creating the user interface, and implementing file listing with Tauri's APIs.(no content)The recommended approach is to first create a basic React application with Typescript - generate a Vite project and turn it into a Tauri app.(no content)The tutorial leverages the Tauri APIs package to interact with the file system using imports like `resolve` from "@tauri-apps/api/path".

## Native Look & Feel for macOS

Apps made with Tauri are native apps, but their UI part runs on WebView, so the look and feel tend to be like a web app. The app Jomai was designed to behave as much as possible like a native app, and it's currently available only for macOS.(no content)Tauri has `appWindow.theme()` to get the current theme and `appWindow.onThemeChanged()` to monitor change events. Using these, you can change the app to match the OS theme settings. There's an example implementation in React.

## Native Window Controls - tauri-controls

**Tauri Controls** is a library that provides native-looking window controls for Tauri 2 applications. You can enhance the user experience of your Tauri 2 applications with window controls that mimic the identical native controls on the current system.(no content)You can specify the platform as "windows", "macos", or "gnome". If the platform property is not specified, the library will automatically detect the operating system the app is running on and display the appropriate element.(no content)Installation for React: `bun add tauri-controls` with peer dependencies `@tauri-apps/plugin-os @tauri-apps/api` and dev dependencies `clsx tailwind-merge`.

## Tauri UI Libraries for React

**Antd** is a UI library that excels in creating dashboards and feature-rich UIs. However, the components lack a truly native appearance and feel, and it's not the right fit if you're not utilizing React.(no content)**Fluent 2** is Microsoft's design system with ready-to-implement React components. It serves as a suitable option if you want your application to have the appearance of being crafted for Windows 11.

## Architecture Notes

Tauri embeds platform webviews (WebKit for macOS, Webview2 for Windows, and webkit2gtk for Linux) inside a native desktop window written and managed with Rust.(no content)By using the OS's native web renderer, the size of a Tauri app can be as little as 600KB.

Apps made with Tauri are native apps, but their UI part runs on WebView, so the look and feel tend to be like a web app. The app Jomai was designed to behave as much as possible like a native app, and it's currently available only for macOS.(no content)Tauri has `appWindow.theme()` to get the current theme and `appWindow.onThemeChanged()` to monitor change events. Using these, you can change the app to match the OS theme settings. There's an example implementation in React.

## Native Window Controls - tauri-controls

**Tauri Controls** is a library that provides native-looking window controls for Tauri 2 applications. You can enhance the user experience of your Tauri 2 applications with window controls that mimic the identical native controls on the current system.(no content)You can specify the platform as "windows", "macos", or "gnome". If the platform property is not specified, the library will automatically detect the operating system the app is running on and display the appropriate element.(no content)Installation for React: `bun add tauri-controls` with peer dependencies `@tauri-apps/plugin-os @tauri-apps/api` and dev dependencies `clsx tailwind-merge`.

## Tauri UI Libraries for React

**Antd** is a UI library that excels in creating dashboards and feature-rich UIs. However, the components lack a truly native appearance and feel, and it's not the right fit if you're not utilizing React.(no content)**Fluent 2** is Microsoft's design system with ready-to-implement React components. It serves as a suitable option if you want your application to have the appearance of being crafted for Windows 11.

## Architecture Notes

Tauri embeds platform webviews (WebKit for macOS, Webview2 for Windows, and webkit2gtk for Linux) inside a native desktop window written and managed with Rust.(no content)By using the OS's native web renderer, the size of a Tauri app can be as little as 600KB.

Encontrei ótimas opções! Vou resumir para o usuário:

1. **xterm.js** - Terminal completo para web, usado pelo VS Code
2. **react-xtermjs** (Qovery) - Wrapper React moderno para xterm.js
3. **Tauri** - Framework Rust + Web para apps desktop nativos
4. **tauri-controls** - Controles de janela nativos do macOS para Tauri
5. **react-desktop** - Componentes React com aparência macOS

Esta abordagem seria muito melhor porque:
- xterm.js é um terminal completo e testado (usado no VS Code!)
- Podemos desenvolver em TypeScript/React (mais familiar)
- Tauri gera apps nativos pequenos (~600KB)
- Existem libs para aparência nativa do macOS

content.text:
Excelente pergunta! Sim, há opções muito melhores:

## Stack Recomendada: Tauri + React + xterm.js

| Componente | Lib | Descrição |
|------------|-----|-----------|
| **Framework** | [Tauri 2.0](https://v2.tauri.app/) | Rust + WebView, apps ~600KB |
| **Terminal** | [xterm.js](https://xtermjs.org/) | Usado pelo VS Code, Hyper, etc |
| **React wrapper** | [react-xtermjs](https://github.com/Qovery/react-xtermjs) | Hooks modernos para xterm |
| **Window controls** | [tauri-controls](https://github.com/agmmnn/tauri-controls) | Botões nativos macOS/Windows |
| **UI macOS** | [react-desktop](https://reactdesktop.js.org/) | Componentes estilo macOS |

## Vantagens

1. **xterm.js** - Terminal completo com suporte a:
   - Atalhos nativos (Cmd+C, Cmd+V)
   - GPU rendering
   - Mouse events
   - Unicode/emojis
   - Funciona com bash, zsh, vim, tmux

2. **Tauri** - Usa WebKit nativo do macOS (não Chromium)
   - Apps muito leves
   - Acesso ao filesystem via Rust
   - Segurança melhor que Electron

3. **Desenvolvimento mais rápido** - TypeScript + React é muito mais produtivo

## Existe até um Finder clone em React
[macOS finder clone built with React](https://reactjsexample.com/macos-finder-clone-built-with-react/)

---

**Quer que eu crie o projeto com Tauri + React + xterm.js?**

file.content:
[package]
name = "tauri-app"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "A Tauri App"
authors = ["you"]
edition = "2021"

# See more keys and their definitions at https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/manifest.html

[lib]
# The `_lib` suffix may seem redundant but it is necessary
# to make the lib name unique and wouldn't conflict with the bin name.
# This seems to be only an issue on Windows, see https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/8519
name = "tauri_app_lib"
crate-type = ["staticlib", "cdylib", "rlib"]

[build-dependencies]
tauri-build = { version = "2", features = [] }

[dependencies]
tauri = { version = "2", features = [] }
tauri-plugin-opener = "2"
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1"

1→// Learn more about Tauri commands at https://tauri.app/develop/calling-rust/
     2→#[tauri::command]
     3→fn greet(name: &str) -> String {
     4→    format!("Hello, {}! You've been greeted from Rust!", name)
     5→}
     6→
     7→#[cfg_attr(mobile, tauri::mobile_entry_point)]
     8→pub fn run() {
     9→    tauri::Builder::default()
    10→        .plugin(tauri_plugin_opener::init())
    11→        .invoke_handler(tauri::generate_handler![greet])
    12→        .run(tauri::generate_context!())
    13→        .expect("error while running tauri application");
    14→}

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The conversation is summarized below:
Analysis:
Let me chronologically analyze the conversation:

1. **Initial Request**: User asked to create an application called "md" (macOS devtools), create folder, init git, implement Anthropic's Harness framework, run fignore for gitignore. The goal was to create a macOS extension that adds a sidebar to Finder windows with a chat/terminal interface that syncs with the current directory.

2. **First Implementation (Node.js/TypeScript with Anthropic SDK)**:
   - Created project at `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/md`
   - Initialized git, ran fignore
   - Discovered "Harness" framework is now called Claude Agent SDK
   - Installed `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `zod`
   - Created bash tool, finder tool, and MDAgent with AI integration
   - User tested CLI and reported it worked but complained about AI integration - wanted pure terminal, not AI chat

3. **Finder POC**:
   - Created POC to test Finder integration via AppleScript
   - Tests showed Finder navigation works
   - User couldn't see the demo initially, so created finder-demo.ts with visible navigation
   - User confirmed cd worked (Finder changed directories)

4. **User showed screenshot**: Wanted terminal displayed alongside Finder as a sidebar

5. **Research on Finder integration options**:
   - Searched for hacks (SIMBL, MacForge, TotalFinder-style injection)
   - User said to ignore hacks, create plan without them
   - Concluded: Native app in Swift is the best viable option

6. **Swift App (MDApp)**:
   - Created SwiftUI app at `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/md/MDApp`
   - Implemented file browser, sidebar, PTY-based terminal
   - Fixed compilation errors (NSHost -> Host, fork() unavailable -> Process)
   - App compiled and ran successfully
   - User confirmed it worked but reported bugs: shortcuts don't work, cd doesn't work, appearance issues

7. **Pivot to Tauri + React**:
   - User asked about using TypeScript-based alternatives
   - Suggested using Ring UI from JetBrains
   - Created new project at `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/md-tauri`
   - Set up Tauri 2.0 with React + TypeScript
   - Installed: `@jetbrains/ring-ui`, `@xterm/xterm`, `@xterm/addon-fit`, `@xterm/addon-web-links`, `@tauri-apps/plugin-shell`, `@tauri-apps/plugin-fs`

8. **Tauri App Issues**:
   - Shell plugin config error: "unknown field `scope`, expected `open`" - Fixed by changing config
   - Ring UI CSS import error: "Failed to resolve import '@jetbrains/ring-ui/components/style.css'" - Fixed by using correct path
   - Ring UI ESM compatibility error: "Importing binding name 'default' cannot be resolved by star export entries"
   - Current fix: Removing Ring UI dependencies and using native HTML/CSS components

Most recent work: Simplifying components to remove Ring UI imports that cause ESM errors.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user wants to create "MD" (macOS DevTools) - a file browser application with an integrated terminal sidebar. The terminal should sync bidirectionally with the file browser: when user runs `cd` in terminal, the file browser updates, and when clicking folders, the terminal's working directory changes. Initially wanted a Finder extension, but due to macOS limitations, pivoted to a standalone native app. Started with Swift, then pivoted to Tauri + React + TypeScript with Ring UI and xterm.js for better development velocity and working terminal.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Tauri 2.0 (Rust + WebView for desktop apps)
   - xterm.js (terminal emulator for web)
   - Ring UI (JetBrains React component library) - having ESM compatibility issues
   - PTY (Pseudo-terminal) for real shell integration
   - AppleScript for Finder control (POC only)
   - Bidirectional sync between file browser and terminal
   - Tauri plugin system (shell, fs, opener plugins)

3. Files and Code Sections:

   **Tauri Project: `/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/md-tauri/`**
   
   - `src-tauri/Cargo.toml` - Added shell and fs plugins:
     ```toml
     [dependencies]
     tauri = { version = "2", features = [] }
     tauri-plugin-opener = "2"
     tauri-plugin-shell = "2"
     tauri-plugin-fs = "2"
     ```

   - `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` - Rust backend with plugins:
     ```rust
     use std::env;

     #[tauri::command]
     fn get_home_dir() -> String {
         env::var("HOME").unwrap_or_else(|_| "/".to_string())
     }

     #[cfg_attr(mobile, tauri::mobile_entry_point)]
     pub fn run() {
         tauri::Builder::default()
             .plugin(tauri_plugin_opener::init())
             .plugin(tauri_plugin_shell::init())
             .plugin(tauri_plugin_fs::init())
             .invoke_handler(tauri::generate_handler![get_home_dir])
             .run(tauri::generate_context!())
             .expect("error while running tauri application");
     }
     ```

   - `src-tauri/capabilities/default.json` - Permissions:
     ```json
     {
       "$schema": "../gen/schemas/desktop-schema.json",
       "identifier": "default",
       "description": "Capability for the main window",
       "windows": ["main"],
       "permissions": [
         "core:default",
         "opener:default",
         "shell:default",
         "shell:allow-spawn",
         "shell:allow-stdin-write",
         "shell:allow-kill",
         "shell:allow-open",
         "shell:allow-execute",
         "fs:default",
         "fs:allow-read-dir",
         "fs:allow-read-file",
         "fs:allow-stat",
         "fs:allow-exists"
       ]
     }
     ```

   - `src-tauri/tauri.conf.json` - App configuration:
     ```json
     {
       "productName": "MD",
       "version": "0.1.0",
       "identifier": "com.md.devtools",
       "app": {
         "windows": [{
           "title": "MD - macOS DevTools",
           "width": 1200,
           "height": 700,
           "minWidth": 800,
           "minHeight": 500
         }]
       },
       "plugins": {
         "shell": { "open": true }
       }
     }
     ```

   - `src/App.tsx` - Main app component (simplified, removing Ring UI):

Based on the search results, here's what I found about `@jetbrains/ring-ui-built` and Vite integration:

## Key Findings

Ring UI is a collection of UI components that aims to provide all the necessary building blocks for web-based products built inside JetBrains, as well as third-party plugins developed for JetBrains' products.

### Using the Pre-built Package (Recommended for Vite)

For the pre-built version `@jetbrains/ring-ui-built`, you need to import RingUI styles once with `import '@jetbrains/ring-ui-built/components/style.css'`, then import components like `import Button from '@jetbrains/ring-ui-built/components/button/button'`. The bundle size will depend on the amount of components you imported.

### Webpack vs Vite

The official documentation primarily covers webpack configuration. For complex builds where you want to compile RingUI sources together with your sources in the same build process, they recommend installing `@jetbrains/ring-ui` with `npm install @jetbrains/ring-ui --save-exact` and using webpack configuration.

### Vite Compatibility Signs

The Ring UI project itself uses Vite-related tooling in development, including `@vitejs/plugin-react` (currently being updated to version 5.0.3) and rollup.

## Recommendation for Vite

Since there's no specific Vite integration guide in the search results, the best approach for Vite projects is to use **`@jetbrains/ring-ui-built`** (the pre-compiled version) rather than `@jetbrains/ring-ui`, as it doesn't require special webpack loader configuration and should work directly with Vite's standard module resolution.

Would you like me to search for more specific Vite configuration examples or troubleshooting information?

input.content:
# Notas para Claude - Referência Futura

## Ring UI da JetBrains

### IMPORTANTE: Existem DUAS versões do Ring UI

1. **`@jetbrains/ring-ui`** - Versão source que REQUER configuração de webpack
   - Não funciona com Vite diretamente
   - Precisa de loaders específicos do webpack

2. **`@jetbrains/ring-ui-built`** - Versão PRÉ-COMPILADA
   - Funciona com Vite, Parcel, e outros bundlers
   - Não precisa de configuração especial
   - Importar estilos uma vez: `import '@jetbrains/ring-ui-built/components/style.css'`
   - Importar componentes: `import Button from '@jetbrains/ring-ui-built/components/button/button'`

### Para projetos Vite/Tauri: SEMPRE usar `@jetbrains/ring-ui-built`

### Documentação
- GitHub: https://github.com/JetBrains/ring-ui
- Storybook: https://jetbrains.github.io/ring-ui/
- NPM: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@jetbrains/ring-ui-built

## Regras Gerais

input.content:
# Plano: QA e Debugging Iterativo em Tempo Real

## Problema Atual
Erro: `SyntaxError: Importing binding name 'open' is not found.`
Preciso de capacidade de debugar e fazer QA de forma iterativa e em tempo real.

---

## Opções de Ferramentas para QA Iterativo

### 1. **Playwright (Recomendado para Web/Tauri)**
- **O que é**: Framework de automação de browser que permite interação programática
- **Capacidades**:
  - Abrir browser em modo headed (visível) ou headless
  - Executar JavaScript no contexto da página
  - Capturar console.log, erros, network requests
  - Interagir com elementos (click, type, etc.)
  - Screenshots e vídeos
- **Iterativo**: Sim - pode manter browser aberto e executar comandos via REPL ou scripts
- **Instalação**: `bun add -d playwright @playwright/test && npx playwright install`
- **Uso para Tauri**: Conectar ao localhost:1420 (servidor Vite)

### 2. **Puppeteer**
- Similar ao Playwright, mais antigo
- Apenas Chrome/Chromium
- **Instalação**: `bun add -d puppeteer`

### 3. **Cypress**
- Framework de teste E2E com UI própria
- **Capacidades**: Dashboard visual, time-travel debugging
- **Iterativo**: Sim - Cypress UI permite ver testes em tempo real
- **Instalação**: `bun add -d cypress`

### 4. **WebDriverIO**
- Suporta múltiplos browsers
- Pode conectar a apps desktop via Appium
- **Instalação**: `bun add -d webdriverio`

---

## Opções de MCP (Model Context Protocol)

### 5. **Playwright MCP**
- **Servidor MCP** que expõe Playwright como ferramenta
- Permite que eu (Claude) controle browser diretamente
- **Capacidades**: Navegar, clicar, ler DOM, executar JS, screenshots
- **Repositório**: github.com/anthropics/mcp-servers (verificar disponibilidade)

### 6. **Browser Automation MCP**
- MCP genérico para automação de browser
- Pode usar Puppeteer ou Playwright como backend

### 7. **macOS Automation MCP**
- Poderia usar AppleScript/JXA para controlar apps nativos
- Útil para testar apps Tauri como app nativo (não via WebView)

---

## Opções de Debugging Direto

### 8. **Safari Web Inspector (para Tauri)**
- Tauri usa WebKit no macOS
- Habilitar: `tauri.conf.json` → `"devtools": true`
- Abrir Safari → Develop → [nome do app]
- **Limitação**: Eu não consigo ver o Safari diretamente

### 9. **Console Remoto via WebSocket**
- Criar servidor WebSocket que recebe logs do app
- App envia `console.log` via WebSocket
- Eu leio o output do servidor
- **Iterativo**: Sim - logs em tempo real

### 10. **Vite Plugin para Logging**
- Plugin que intercepta erros e envia para arquivo/servidor
- Eu monitoro o arquivo

---

## Opções de Teste Visual

### 11. **Storybook**
- Desenvolver componentes isoladamente
- Visual testing integrado
- **Instalação**: `npx storybook@latest init`

### 12. **Chromatic / Percy**
- Visual regression testing
- Compara screenshots entre versões

---

## Solução Proposta: Playwright Direto

### Por que Playwright:
1. Posso executar scripts e ver resultados imediatamente
2. Posso manter sessão aberta e executar comandos iterativamente
3. Captura erros de console automaticamente
4. Funciona com localhost:1420 (Vite dev server)
5. Não precisa de MCP - funciona via CLI

### Fluxo de Trabalho Proposto:

```
1. Iniciar Tauri dev (background)
   └── App roda em localhost:1420

2. Criar script Playwright de debug
   └── Conecta ao localhost:1420
   └── Captura console.log/errors
   └── Exporta para arquivo que eu leio

3. Loop iterativo:
   a. Eu leio arquivo de logs
   b. Identifico erro
   c. Corrijo código
   d. HMR atualiza app
   e. Playwright detecta mudança
   f. Volta para (a)
```

### Script de Debug Exemplo:

```typescript
// debug.ts
import { chromium } from 'playwright';

const browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: false });
const page = await browser.newPage();

// Capturar todos os logs
page.on('console', msg => {
  console.log(`[${msg.type()}] ${msg.text()}`);
});

page.on('pageerror', err => {
  console.log(`[ERROR] ${err.message}`);
});

await page.goto('http://localhost:1420');

// Manter aberto para debugging
await page.waitForTimeout(3600000); // 1 hora
```

---

## Decisão Necessária

Qual abordagem você prefere?

1. **Playwright direto** - Instalar e criar scripts de teste
2. **Playwright MCP** - Se disponível, configurar como MCP server
3. **Console remoto** - Criar WebSocket para logs em tempo real
4. **Outra opção** - Das listadas acima

---

## Notas

- Nenhuma das opções acima altera configurações do sistema
- Playwright instala browsers em sandbox próprio (~/.cache/ms-playwright)
- Todas são dev dependencies (não vão para produção)

content.content:
# Automation MCP Server Overview

## Purpose
Automation MCP is a Model Context Protocol server enabling AI assistants to automate macOS desktop interactions through comprehensive control systems.

## Core Capabilities

**Input Control:**
- Mouse operations: clicking, movement, scrolling, dragging, path following
- Keyboard input: text typing, key combinations, system shortcuts
- Window management: focus, positioning, resizing, minimization

**Screen Interaction:**
- Full/partial/window-specific screenshot capture
- Pixel color analysis
- Visual region highlighting
- Template-based image detection (waiting for UI elements to appear)
- Display dimension retrieval

## Installation

**Requirements:**
Bun runtime (JavaScript-compatible environment)

**Setup steps:**
```
git clone https://github.com/ashwwwin/automation-mcp.git
cd automation-mcp
bun install
```

**Launch options:**
- HTTP transport: `bun run index.ts`
- Stdio transport: `bun run index.ts --stdio`

## System Requirements

macOS requires explicit permission grants:
- **Accessibility:** enables mouse/keyboard control and window operations
- **Screen Recording:** enables screenshot and visual analysis features

## Real-time Automation Testing

The server facilitates iterative testing through:
- Immediate screenshot verification of UI state changes
- Synchronous image detection for conditional logic
- Direct manipulation of UI coordinates
- State validation via color detection at specific positions

This enables AI models to observe execution results and adapt subsequent actions dynamically.

## Integration
Works with Claude Desktop via MCP configuration, supporting both furi wrapper and direct bun execution methods.

content.content:
# Automation MCP: Screen Recording & Visual Analysis Capabilities

## Screen Recording & Analysis Tools

The MCP server provides comprehensive visual analysis through these functions:

**Screenshot Capabilities:**
- `screenshot` - "Capture full screen, regions, or specific windows"
- `screenInfo` - Retrieve screen dimensions
- `screenHighlight` - Visually mark screen regions
- `colorAt` - Detect pixel colors at any location
- `waitForImage` - "Wait for images to appear (template matching)"

Screen recording permissions enable these features, allowing the system to capture and analyze visual content in real-time.

## Complete Tool Arsenal

**Mouse Operations (8 functions):**
`mouseClick`, `mouseDoubleClick`, `mouseMove`, `mouseGetPosition`, `mouseScroll`, `mouseDrag`, `mouseButtonControl`, `mouseMovePath`

**Keyboard Control (3 functions):**
`type`, `keyControl`, `systemCommand` for shortcuts (copy, paste, undo, save)

**Window Management (3 functions):**
`getWindows`, `getActiveWindow`, `windowControl` for focus/move/resize/minimize operations

## Required Permissions

The system mandates two macOS permissions:
1. **Accessibility** - enables mouse/keyboard/window control
2. **Screen Recording** - enables screenshot and visual analysis functions

The platform runs exclusively on macOS with Bun runtime and requires explicit user authorization for full functionality.

Entendi. Vou analisar a viabilidade do **automation-mcp**:

## Análise de Viabilidade: automation-mcp

### Capacidades Relevantes
| Ferramenta | Descrição | Iterativo |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| `screenshot` | Captura tela/região/janela | Sim - sob demanda |
| `waitForImage` | Template matching - espera elemento aparecer | Sim - polling |
| `colorAt` | Detecta cor em pixel específico | Sim - validação |
| `mouseClick/Move/Drag` | Controle do mouse | Sim |
| `type/keyControl` | Controle do teclado | Sim |
| `windowControl` | Foco, posição, resize | Sim |

### Requisitos
- **macOS**: Sim (seu sistema)
- **Bun**: Sim (já instalado)
- **Permissões**: Accessibility + Screen Recording

### Fluxo Iterativo Possível
```
Loop:
  1. Eu executo ação (click, type, etc.)
  2. Eu capturo tela (via screen recording permission)
  3. Eu analiso resultado visual
  4. Eu decido próxima ação
  → Volta para 1
```

### Veredito
**VIÁVEL** - Permite iteração real-time porque:
- Capturas são sob demanda (não screenshots batch)
- `waitForImage` permite aguardar estados específicos
- Controle completo de input permite interagir com qualquer app
- Funciona com Tauri (app nativo macOS)

### Próximo Passo
Deseja que eu clone e configure o automation-mcp? Precisará conceder permissões de Accessibility e Screen Recording nas Preferências do Sistema.

file.content:
# 🤖 Automation MCP

Automation MCP is a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that provides AI models with **complete desktop automation capabilities** on macOS. It enables AI assistants to:

- 🖱️ **Control your mouse** (click, move, scroll, drag)
- ⌨️ **Type and send keyboard input** (including system shortcuts)
- 📸 **Take screenshots** and analyze screen content
- 🪟 **Manage windows** (focus, move, resize, minimize)
- 🎯 **Interact with UI elements** through coordinates
- 🎨 **Analyze screen colors** and highlight regions
- 🔍 **Wait for images** to appear on screen

## 🚀 Quick start

Make sure you have [furi](https://github.com/ashwwwin/furi) installed, and then run the following command:

```bash
furi add ashwwwin/automation-mcp
```

followed by:

```bash
furi start ashwwwin/automation-mcp
```

and you're done! (or you can just use the furi desktop app for no cli).

## 🥲 Normal start (without furi)

### Prerequisites

- **Bun** runtime - Install with: `curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash`

### 1. Clone and Install

```bash
git clone https://github.com/ashwwwin/automation-mcp.git
cd automation-mcp
bun install
```

### 2. Start the Server

```bash
# Start with HTTP transport (recommended for web apps)
bun run index.ts

# Or start with stdio transport (for command line tools)
bun run index.ts --stdio
```

### 3. Grant Permissions

On first run, macOS will ask for permissions. **You must grant these** for full functionality:

1. **Accessibility** - Allows keyboard/mouse control
2. **Screen Recording** - Enables screenshots and screen analysis

Or manually enable in: **System Settings** → **Privacy & Security** → **Accessibility/Screen Recording**

## 🛠️ Available Tools

### 🖱️ Mouse Control

- `mouseClick` - Click at coordinates with left/right/middle button
- `mouseDoubleClick` - Double-click at coordinates
- `mouseMove` - Move cursor to position
- `mouseGetPosition` - Get current cursor location
- `mouseScroll` - Scroll in any direction
- `mouseDrag` - Drag from current position to target
- `mouseButtonControl` - Press/release mouse buttons
- `mouseMovePath` - Follow a smooth path with multiple points

### ⌨️ Keyboard Input

- `type` - Type text or press key combinations
- `keyControl` - Advanced key press/release control
- `systemCommand` - Common shortcuts (copy, paste, undo, save, etc.)

### 📸 Screen Capture & Analysis

- `screenshot` - Capture full screen, regions, or specific windows
- `screenInfo` - Get screen dimensions
- `screenHighlight` - Highlight screen regions visually
- `colorAt` - Get color of any pixel
- `waitForImage` - Wait for images to appear (template matching)

### 🪟 Window Management

- `getWindows` - List all open windows
- `getActiveWindow` - Get current active window
- `windowControl` - Focus, move, resize, minimize windows

## 🔒 Security & Permissions

1. **Accessibility** - Required for:

   - Mouse clicks and movement
   - Keyboard input simulation
   - Window management

2. **Screen Recording** - Required for:
   - Taking screenshots
   - Screen analysis
   - Color detection

## 🚀 Integration Examples

### With Claude Desktop + furi

If you've already configured furi with Claude Desktop, you don't need to do anything.

Add to your MCP configuration:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "furi": {
      "command": "furi",
      "args": ["connect"]
    }
  }
}
```

### With Claude Desktop

Add to your MCP configuration:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "automation": {
      "command": "bun",
      "args": ["run", "/path/to/automation-mcp/index.ts", "--stdio"]
    }
  }
}
```

## 🐛 Troubleshooting

### Common Issues

**Permission Denied Errors**

- Ensure Accessibility and Screen Recording permissions are granted
- Ensure Xcode Command Line Tools: `xcode-select --install`

## 🙋‍♂️ Support

Having issues? Check the troubleshooting section above or open an issue with:

- Your operating system and version
- Error messages
- Steps to reproduce

## 2.0.69

- Minor bugfixes

## 2.0.68

- Fixed IME (Input Method Editor) support for languages like Chinese, Japanese, and Korean by correctly positioning the composition window at the cursor
- Fixed a bug where disallowed MCP tools were visible to the model
- Fixed an issue where steering messages could be lost while a subagent is working
- Fixed Option+Arrow word navigation treating entire CJK (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) text sequences as a single word instead of navigating by word boundaries
- Improved plan mode exit UX: show simplified yes/no dialog when exiting with empty or missing plan instead of throwing an error
- Add support for enterprise managed settings. Contact your Anthropic account team to enable this feature.

## 2.0.67

- Thinking mode is now enabled by default for Opus 4.5
- Thinking mode configuration has moved to /config
- Added search functionality to `/permissions` command with `/` keyboard shortcut for filtering rules by tool name
- Show reason why autoupdater is disabled in `/doctor`
- Fixed false "Another process is currently updating Claude" error when running `claude update` while another instance is already on the latest version
- Fixed MCP servers from `.mcp.json` being stuck in pending state when running in non-interactive mode (`-p` flag or piped input)
- Fixed scroll position resetting after deleting a permission rule in `/permissions`
- Fixed word deletion (opt+delete) and word navigation (opt+arrow) not working correctly with non-Latin text such as Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic, Hebrew, Thai, and Chinese
- Fixed `claude install --force` not bypassing stale lock files
- Fixed consecutive @~/ file references in CLAUDE.md being incorrectly parsed due to markdown strikethrough interference
- Windows: Fixed plugin MCP servers failing due to colons in log directory paths

## 2.0.65

- Added ability to switch models while writing a prompt using alt+p (linux, windows), option+p (macos).
- Added context window information to status line input
- Added `fileSuggestion` setting for custom `@` file search commands
- Added `CLAUDE_CODE_SHELL` environment variable to override automatic shell detection (useful when login shell differs from actual working shell)
- Fixed prompt not being saved to history when aborting a query with Escape
- Fixed Read tool image handling to identify format from bytes instead of file extension

## 2.0.64

- Made auto-compacting instant
- Agents and bash commands can run asynchronously and send messages to wake up the main agent
- /stats now provides users with interesting CC stats, such as favorite model, usage graph, usage streak
- Added named session support: use `/rename` to name sessions, `/resume <name>` in REPL or `claude --resume <name>` from the terminal to resume them
- Added support for .claude/rules/`.  See https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory for details.
- Added image dimension metadata when images are resized, enabling accurate coordinate mappings for large images
- Fixed auto-loading .env when using native installer
- Fixed `--system-prompt` being ignored when using `--continue` or `--resume` flags
- Improved `/resume` screen with grouped forked sessions and keyboard shortcuts for preview (P) and rename (R)
- VSCode: Added copy-to-clipboard button on code blocks and bash tool inputs
- VSCode: Fixed extension not working on Windows ARM64 by falling back to x64 binary via emulation
- Bedrock: Improve efficiency of token counting
- Bedrock: Add support for `aws login` AWS Management Console credentials
- Unshipped AgentOutputTool and BashOutputTool, in favor of a new unified TaskOutputTool

## 2.0.62

- Added "(Recommended)" indicator for multiple-choice questions, with the recommended option moved to the top of the list
- Added `attribution` setting to customize commit and PR bylines (deprecates `includeCoAuthoredBy`)
- Fixed duplicate slash commands appearing when ~/.claude is symlinked to a project directory
- Fixed slash command selection not working when multiple commands share the same name
- Fixed an issue where skill files inside symlinked skill directories could become circular symlinks
- Fixed running versions getting removed because lock file incorrectly going stale
- Fixed IDE diff tab not closing when rejecting file changes

## 2.0.61

- Reverted VSCode support for multiple terminal clients due to responsiveness issues.

- Added background agent support. Agents run in the background while you work

- Enabled "/mcp enable [server-name]" or "/mcp disable [server-name]" to quickly toggle all servers
- Updated Fetch to skip summarization for pre-approved websites
- VSCode: Added support for multiple terminal clients connecting to the IDE server simultaneously

## 2.0.59

- Added --agent CLI flag to override the agent setting for the current session
- Added `agent` setting to configure main thread with a specific agent's system prompt, tool restrictions, and model

- Added `CLAUDE_CODE_SHELL_PREFIX` for wrapping Claude and user-provided shell commands run by Claude Code

## 1.0.60

- You can now create custom subagents for specialized tasks! Run /agents to get started

## 1.0.59

- SDK: Added tool confirmation support with canUseTool callback
- SDK: Allow specifying env for spawned process
- Hooks: Exposed PermissionDecision to hooks (including "ask")
- Hooks: UserPromptSubmit now supports additionalContext in advanced JSON output
- Fixed issue where some Max users that specified Opus would still see fallback to Sonnet

## 1.0.58

- Added support for reading PDFs
- MCP: Improved server health status display in 'claude mcp list'
- Hooks: Added CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR env var for hook commands

## 1.0.57

- Added support for specifying a model in slash commands
- Improved permission messages to help Claude understand allowed tools
- Fix: Remove trailing newlines from bash output in terminal wrapping

## 1.0.56

- Windows: Enabled shift+tab for mode switching on versions of Node.js that support terminal VT mode
- Fixes for WSL IDE detection
- Fix an issue causing awsRefreshHelper changes to .aws directory not to be picked up

## 1.0.55

- Clarified knowledge cutoff for Opus 4 and Sonnet 4 models
- Windows: fixed Ctrl+Z crash
- SDK: Added ability to capture error logging
- Add --system-prompt-file option to override system prompt in print mode

## 1.0.54

- Hooks: Added UserPromptSubmit hook and the current working directory to hook inputs
- Custom slash commands: Added argument-hint to frontmatter
- Windows: OAuth uses port 45454 and properly constructs browser URL
- Windows: mode switching now uses alt + m, and plan mode renders properly
- Shell: Switch to in-memory shell snapshot to fix file-related errors

## 1.0.53

- Updated @-mention file truncation from 100 lines to 2000 lines
- Add helper script settings for AWS token refresh: awsAuthRefresh (for foreground operations like aws sso login) and awsCredentialExport (for background operation with STS-like response).

## 1.0.52

- Added support for MCP server instructions

## 1.0.51

- Added support for native Windows (requires Git for Windows)
- Added support for Bedrock API keys through environment variable AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK
- Settings: /doctor can now help you identify and fix invalid setting files
- `--append-system-prompt` can now be used in interactive mode, not just --print/-p.
- Increased auto-compact warning threshold from 60% to 80%
- Fixed an issue with handling user directories with spaces for shell snapshots
- OTEL resource now includes os.type, os.version, host.arch, and wsl.version (if running on Windows Subsystem for Linux)
- Custom slash commands: Fixed user-level commands in subdirectories
- Plan mode: Fixed issue where rejected plan from sub-task would get discarded

## 1.0.48

- Fixed a bug in v1.0.45 where the app would sometimes freeze on launch
- Added progress messages to Bash tool based on the last 5 lines of command output
- Added expanding variables support for MCP server configuration
- Moved shell snapshots from /tmp to ~/.claude for more reliable Bash tool calls
- Improved IDE extension path handling when Claude Code runs in WSL
- Hooks: Added a PreCompact hook
- Vim mode: Added c, f/F, t/T

## 1.0.45

- Redesigned Search (Grep) tool with new tool input parameters and features
- Disabled IDE diffs for notebook files, fixing "Timeout waiting after 1000ms" error
- Fixed config file corruption issue by enforcing atomic writes
- Updated prompt input undo to Ctrl+\_ to avoid breaking existing Ctrl+U behavior, matching zsh's undo shortcut
- Stop Hooks: Fixed transcript path after /clear and fixed triggering when loop ends with tool call
- Custom slash commands: Restored namespacing in command names based on subdirectories. For example, .claude/commands/frontend/component.md is now /frontend:component, not /component.

## 1.0.44

- New /export command lets you quickly export a conversation for sharing
- MCP: resource_link tool results are now supported
- MCP: tool annotations and tool titles now display in /mcp view
- Changed Ctrl+Z to suspend Claude Code. Resume by running `fg`. Prompt input undo is now Ctrl+U.

## 1.0.43

- Fixed a bug where the theme selector was saving excessively
- Hooks: Added EPIPE system error handling

## 1.0.42

- Added tilde (`~`) expansion support to `/add-dir` command

## 1.0.41

- Hooks: Split Stop hook triggering into Stop and SubagentStop
- Hooks: Enabled optional timeout configuration for each command
- Hooks: Added "hook_event_name" to hook input
- Fixed a bug where MCP tools would display twice in tool list
- New tool parameters JSON for Bash tool in `tool_decision` event

## 1.0.40

- Fixed a bug causing API connection errors with UNABLE_TO_GET_ISSUER_CERT_LOCALLY if `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` was set

## 1.0.39

- New Active Time metric in OpenTelemetry logging

## 1.0.38

- Released hooks. Special thanks to community input in https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/712. Docs: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

## 1.0.37

- Remove ability to set `Proxy-Authorization` header via ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN or apiKeyHelper

## 1.0.36

- Web search now takes today's date into context
- Fixed a bug where stdio MCP servers were not terminating properly on exit

## 1.0.35

- Added support for MCP OAuth Authorization Server discovery

## 1.0.34

- Fixed a memory leak causing a MaxListenersExceededWarning message to appear

## 1.0.33

- Improved logging functionality with session ID support
- Added prompt input undo functionality (Ctrl+Z and vim 'u' command)
- Improvements to plan mode

## 1.0.32

- Updated loopback config for litellm
- Added forceLoginMethod setting to bypass login selection screen

## 1.0.31

- Fixed a bug where ~/.claude.json would get reset when file contained invalid JSON

## 1.0.30

- Custom slash commands: Run bash output, @-mention files, enable thinking with thinking keywords
- Improved file path autocomplete with filename matching
- Added timestamps in Ctrl-r mode and fixed Ctrl-c handling
- Enhanced jq regex support for complex filters with pipes and select

## 1.0.29

- Improved CJK character support in cursor navigation and rendering

## 1.0.28

- Slash commands: Fix selector display during history navigation
- Resizes images before upload to prevent API size limit errors
- Added XDG_CONFIG_HOME support to configuration directory
- Performance optimizations for memory usage
- New attributes (terminal.type, language) in OpenTelemetry logging

## 1.0.27

- Streamable HTTP MCP servers are now supported
- Remote MCP servers (SSE and HTTP) now support OAuth
- MCP resources can now be @-mentioned
- /resume slash command to switch conversations within Claude Code

## 1.0.25

- Slash commands: moved "project" and "user" prefixes to descriptions
- Slash commands: improved reliability for command discovery
- Improved support for Ghostty
- Improved web search reliability

## 1.0.24

- Improved /mcp output
- Fixed a bug where settings arrays got overwritten instead of merged

## 1.0.23

- Released TypeScript SDK: import @anthropic-ai/claude-code to get started
- Released Python SDK: pip install claude-code-sdk to get started

## 1.0.22

- SDK: Renamed `total_cost` to `total_cost_usd`

## 1.0.21

- Improved editing of files with tab-based indentation
- Fix for tool_use without matching tool_result errors
- Fixed a bug where stdio MCP server processes would linger after quitting Claude Code

## 1.0.18

- Added --add-dir CLI argument for specifying additional working directories
- Added streaming input support without require -p flag
- Improved startup performance and session storage performance
- Added CLAUDE_BASH_MAINTAIN_PROJECT_WORKING_DIR environment variable to freeze working directory for bash commands
- Added detailed MCP server tools display (/mcp)
- MCP authentication and permission improvements
- Added auto-reconnection for MCP SSE connections on disconnect
- Fixed issue where pasted content was lost when dialogs appeared

## 1.0.17

- We now emit messages from sub-tasks in -p mode (look for the parent_tool_use_id property)
- Fixed crashes when the VS Code diff tool is invoked multiple times quickly
- MCP server list UI improvements
- Update Claude Code process title to display "claude" instead of "node"

## 1.0.11

- Claude Code can now also be used with a Claude Pro subscription
- Added /upgrade for smoother switching to Claude Max plans
- Improved UI for authentication from API keys and Bedrock/Vertex/external auth tokens
- Improved shell configuration error handling
- Improved todo list handling during compaction

## 1.0.10

- Added markdown table support
- Improved streaming performance

## 1.0.8

- Fixed Vertex AI region fallback when using CLOUD_ML_REGION
- Increased default otel interval from 1s -> 5s
- Fixed edge cases where MCP_TIMEOUT and MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT weren't being respected
- Fixed a regression where search tools unnecessarily asked for permissions
- Added support for triggering thinking non-English languages
- Improved compacting UI

## 1.0.7

- Renamed /allowed-tools -> /permissions
- Migrated allowedTools and ignorePatterns from .claude.json -> settings.json
- Deprecated claude config commands in favor of editing settings.json
- Fixed a bug where --dangerously-skip-permissions sometimes didn't work in --print mode
- Improved error handling for /install-github-app
- Bugfixes, UI polish, and tool reliability improvements

## 1.0.6

- Improved edit reliability for tab-indented files
- Respect CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR everywhere
- Reduced unnecessary tool permission prompts
- Added support for symlinks in @file typeahead
- Bugfixes, UI polish, and tool reliability improvements

## 1.0.4

- Fixed a bug where MCP tool errors weren't being parsed correctly

## 1.0.1

- Added `DISABLE_INTERLEAVED_THINKING` to give users the option to opt out of interleaved thinking.
- Improved model references to show provider-specific names (Sonnet 3.7 for Bedrock, Sonnet 4 for Console)
- Updated documentation links and OAuth process descriptions

## 1.0.0

- Claude Code is now generally available
- Introducing Sonnet 4 and Opus 4 models

## 0.2.125

- Breaking change: Bedrock ARN passed to `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` or `ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL` should no longer contain an escaped slash (specify `/` instead of `%2F`)
- Removed `DEBUG=true` in favor of `ANTHROPIC_LOG=debug`, to log all requests

## 0.2.117

- Breaking change: --print JSON output now returns nested message objects, for forwards-compatibility as we introduce new metadata fields
- Introduced settings.cleanupPeriodDays
- Introduced CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER_TTL_MS env var
- Introduced --debug mode

## 0.2.108

- You can now send messages to Claude while it works to steer Claude in real-time
- Introduced BASH_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS and BASH_MAX_TIMEOUT_MS env vars
- Fixed a bug where thinking was not working in -p mode
- Fixed a regression in /cost reporting
- Deprecated MCP wizard interface in favor of other MCP commands
- Lots of other bugfixes and improvements

## 0.2.107

- CLAUDE.md files can now import other files. Add @path/to/file.md to ./CLAUDE.md to load additional files on launch

## 0.2.106

- MCP SSE server configs can now specify custom headers
- Fixed a bug where MCP permission prompt didn't always show correctly

## 0.2.105

- Claude can now search the web
- Moved system & account status to /status
- Added word movement keybindings for Vim
- Improved latency for startup, todo tool, and file edits

## 0.2.102

- Improved thinking triggering reliability
- Improved @mention reliability for images and folders
- You can now paste multiple large chunks into one prompt

## 0.2.100

- Fixed a crash caused by a stack overflow error
- Made db storage optional; missing db support disables --continue and --resume

## 0.2.98

- Fixed an issue where auto-compact was running twice

## 0.2.96

- Claude Code can now also be used with a Claude Max subscription (https://claude.ai/upgrade)

## 0.2.93

- Resume conversations from where you left off from with "claude --continue" and "claude --resume"
- Claude now has access to a Todo list that helps it stay on track and be more organized

## 0.2.82

- Added support for --disallowedTools
- Renamed tools for consistency: LSTool -> LS, View -> Read, etc.

## 0.2.75

- Hit Enter to queue up additional messages while Claude is working
- Drag in or copy/paste image files directly into the prompt
- @-mention files to directly add them to context
- Run one-off MCP servers with `claude --mcp-config <path-to-file>`
- Improved performance for filename auto-complete

## 0.2.74

- Added support for refreshing dynamically generated API keys (via apiKeyHelper), with a 5 minute TTL
- Task tool can now perform writes and run bash commands

## 0.2.72

- Updated spinner to indicate tokens loaded and tool usage

## 0.2.70

- Network commands like curl are now available for Claude to use
- Claude can now run multiple web queries in parallel
- Pressing ESC once immediately interrupts Claude in Auto-accept mode

## 0.2.69

- Fixed UI glitches with improved Select component behavior
- Enhanced terminal output display with better text truncation logic

## 0.2.67

- Shared project permission rules can be saved in .claude/settings.json

## 0.2.66

- Print mode (-p) now supports streaming output via --output-format=stream-json
- Fixed issue where pasting could trigger memory or bash mode unexpectedly

## 0.2.63

- Fixed an issue where MCP tools were loaded twice, which caused tool call errors

## 0.2.61

- Navigate menus with vim-style keys (j/k) or bash/emacs shortcuts (Ctrl+n/p) for faster interaction
- Enhanced image detection for more reliable clipboard paste functionality
- Fixed an issue where ESC key could crash the conversation history selector

## 0.2.59

- Copy+paste images directly into your prompt
- Improved progress indicators for bash and fetch tools
- Bugfixes for non-interactive mode (-p)

## 0.2.54

- Quickly add to Memory by starting your message with '#'
- Press ctrl+r to see full output for long tool results
- Added support for MCP SSE transport

## 0.2.53

- New web fetch tool lets Claude view URLs that you paste in
- Fixed a bug with JPEG detection

## 0.2.50

- New MCP "project" scope now allows you to add MCP servers to .mcp.json files and commit them to your repository

## 0.2.49

- Previous MCP server scopes have been renamed: previous "project" scope is now "local" and "global" scope is now "user"

## 0.2.47

- Press Tab to auto-complete file and folder names
- Press Shift + Tab to toggle auto-accept for file edits
- Automatic conversation compaction for infinite conversation length (toggle with /config)

## 0.2.44

- Ask Claude to make a plan with thinking mode: just say 'think' or 'think harder' or even 'ultrathink'

## 0.2.41

- MCP server startup timeout can now be configured via MCP_TIMEOUT environment variable
- MCP server startup no longer blocks the app from starting up

## 0.2.37

- New /release-notes command lets you view release notes at any time
- `claude config add/remove` commands now accept multiple values separated by commas or spaces

## 0.2.36

- Import MCP servers from Claude Desktop with `claude mcp add-from-claude-desktop`
- Add MCP servers as JSON strings with `claude mcp add-json <n> <json>`

## 0.2.34

- Vim bindings for text input - enable with /vim or /config

## 0.2.32

- Interactive MCP setup wizard: Run "claude mcp add" to add MCP servers with a step-by-step interface
- Fix for some PersistentShell issues

## 0.2.31

- Custom slash commands: Markdown files in .claude/commands/ directories now appear as custom slash commands to insert prompts into your conversation
- MCP debug mode: Run with --mcp-debug flag to get more information about MCP server errors

## 0.2.30

- Added ANSI color theme for better terminal compatibility
- Fixed issue where slash command arguments weren't being sent properly
- (Mac-only) API keys are now stored in macOS Keychain

## 0.2.26

- New /approved-tools command for managing tool permissions
- Word-level diff display for improved code readability
- Fuzzy matching for slash commands

## 0.2.21

- Fuzzy matching for /commands
